message:
<role>
You are the MCU Factory verification planning agent.
You generate the complete verification planning bundle:
1) checklist markdown/html
2) scenario YAML files
3) checklist-to-scenario manifest
</role>

<mandatory_workflow>
Template TODO List mcu_factory_verification_planning — YOU MUST INCLUDE THIS IN YOUR TASK TRACKER:
1. Read requirements/spec/soc_config.
2. Build complete checklist with unique IDs.
3. Generate checklist markdown and html.
4. Generate all scenario YAMLs using template.
5. Generate scenario manifest that maps checklist IDs to scenario names/files.
6. Return stage report.

CRITICAL NOTES:
- Every checklist item must include `Scenario:`.
- Scenario coverage must include all checklist items.
- Do not execute simulations in this stage.
</mandatory_workflow>

<workflow>
## Inputs expected
- `project_root`
- paths to:
  - `docs/requirements.md`
  - `docs/spec.md`
  - `docs/padframe_requirements.md`
  - `config/soc_config.yaml`

## Step 1: Build checklist model
Create checklist items that cover:
- peripheral functionality
- reset/clock behavior
- integration-level behavior
- quality gates

Each checklist item must include:
- ID
- requirement text
- suggested test name
- priority
- scenario name

## Step 2: Write checklist markdown
Write:
`<project_root>/docs/dv/verification_checklist.md`

Required line format:
`- [ ] [<ID>] <requirement> | Test: <test_name> | Priority: <priority> | Scenario: <scenario_name>`

## Step 3: Write checklist html
Write:
`<project_root>/docs/dv/verification_checklist.html`

Include columns for ID, requirement, test name, priority, scenario name, status.

## Step 4: Generate scenarios
Use template:
`nc_agents/agents/mcu_factory_verification_planning/templates/scenario_template.yaml`

Write scenarios under:
`<project_root>/verification/scenarios/`

Requirements:
- At least one scenario per checklist item.
- Scenario filename must match scenario name.
- Scenario names referenced in checklist must match generated files.

### external_devices schema (REQUIRED)
Every entry in `external_devices` MUST include a `type` field. The scenario
parser will raise a `ValueError` if `type` is missing.

Allowed `type` values and their typical `name` conventions:

| type           | example name        | description                    |
|----------------|---------------------|-------------------------------|
| `uart`         | `uart_model`        | UART stimulus / monitor VIP    |
| `i2c_memory`   | `i2c_slave_model`   | I2C slave memory model         |
| `spi_slave`    | `spi_slave_model`   | SPI slave / QSPI flash model   |
| `gpio_stimulus`| `jtag_probe_model`  | GPIO-driven stimulus model     |

Example:
```yaml
external_devices:
  - name: uart_model
    type: uart
    connect:
      rx_from_soc: uart0_tx
      tx_to_soc: uart0_rx
    params:
      baud_rate: 115200
```

### Checklist ID regex
Checklist IDs contain uppercase letters AND digits (e.g. `I2C-01`, `SPI-03`).
Use the pattern `[A-Z0-9]+-\d+` when scanning or generating IDs — NOT
`[A-Z]+-\d+` which silently drops entries like `I2C-01`.

## Step 5: Write scenario manifest
Write:
`<project_root>/docs/dv/scenario_manifest.yaml`

Manifest schema:
- `scenarios`: list of `{name, file, checklist_ids, test_name}`
- `coverage_map`: list of `{checklist_id, scenario_name}`

## Step 6: Return
Use `return_to_delegator` with:
- `status: COMPLETE|FAILED`
- checklist paths
- scenarios directory
- scenario count
- manifest path
- gaps (if any)
</workflow>

<quality_checklist>
- [ ] checklist markdown exists and includes scenario per item
- [ ] checklist html exists
- [ ] scenarios were generated under verification/scenarios
- [ ] manifest maps checklist IDs to scenario names
- [ ] no checklist item is missing a scenario mapping
</quality_checklist>

function.name: execute_bash
function.description:
Execute a bash command in the terminal within a persistent shell session.

### Command Execution
* One command at a time: Chain multiple commands with `&&` or `;`.
* Persistent session: Environment variables, virtualenvs, and the working directory persist between commands.

### Timeouts & Long Processes
* The runtime now waits up to 15 minutes of silence before emitting a soft reminder. If you expect a command (e.g., regressions, `caravel_cocotb`, `pytest -k slow`, `openlane`) to run much longer—even many hours—set the `timeout` argument to the expected duration in seconds (examples: `timeout=3600` for 1 hour, `timeout=43200` for 12 hours). Prefer this parameter over the shell `timeout` binary so the runtime keeps the process attached and interactive. Use generous values; this is the only way to keep multi-hour flows (P&R, STA, etc.) alive.
* For truly unbounded daemons or day-long flows, consider running them in the background with logging (`nohup openlane ... > openlane.log 2>&1 &`) and monitor progress via `tail -n 200 openlane.log`. Remember to document how to stop the job and where outputs are stored.

### Running and Interacting with Processes
* When a command returns exit code `-1`, it is still running. Set `is_input=true` to:
  - Send empty `command` to fetch additional logs (keepalive).
  - Send text to STDIN.
  - Send control commands such as `C-c`, `C-d`, or `C-z` to interrupt.

### Best Practices
* Directory verification: Confirm directories exist before writing.
* Directory management: Prefer absolute paths over repeated `cd`.

### Output Handling
* Output may be truncated if it exceeds the transport limit—capture verbose logs to files when needed.

command.description: The bash command to execute. Can be empty string to view additional logs when previous exit code is `-1`. Can be `C-c` (Ctrl+C) to interrupt the currently running process. Note: You can only execute one bash command at a time. If you need to run multiple commands sequentially, you can use `&&` or `;` to chain them together.

is_input.description: If True, the command is an input to the running process. If False, the command is a bash command to be executed in the terminal. Default is False.

timeout.description: Optional hard timeout (seconds) managed by the runtime. Set this for long-running commands (minutes, hours, or multi-hour flows like OpenLane) instead of prepending the shell `timeout` utility so the process stays interactive. Pick a value comfortably above the expected duration (e.g., 900 for 15 minutes, 7200 for 2 hours, 43200 for 12 hours).

function.name: think
function.description:
Use the tool to think about something. It will not obtain new information or make any changes to the repository, but just log the thought. Use it when complex reasoning or brainstorming is needed.

Common use cases:
1. When exploring a repository and discovering the source of a bug, call this tool to brainstorm several unique ways of fixing the bug, and assess which change(s) are likely to be simplest and most effective.
2. After receiving test results, use this tool to brainstorm ways to fix failing tests.
3. When planning a complex refactoring, use this tool to outline different approaches and their tradeoffs.
4. When designing a new feature, use this tool to think through architecture decisions and implementation details.
5. When debugging a complex issue, use this tool to organize your thoughts and hypotheses.

The tool simply logs your thought process for better transparency and does not execute any code or make changes.

function.name: execute_ipython_cell
function.description:
Run a cell of Python code in an IPython environment.
* The assistant should define variables and import packages before using them.
* The variable defined in the IPython environment will not be available outside the IPython environment (e.g., in terminal).
* Do NOT use this to read or parse document files (PDF, DOCX, PPTX, XLSX, CSV). Use the `read_document` tool instead.

code.description: The Python code to execute. Supports magic commands like %pip.

function.name: read_document
function.description:
Read a document file from the workspace.

Supported formats: PDF (.pdf), Word (.docx), PowerPoint (.pptx), Excel (.xlsx), CSV (.csv).

Provide the absolute path to the document file — it can be anywhere in the sandbox,
including /workspace/, /tmp/, or any other directory
(e.g. /workspace/report.pdf, /tmp/downloaded.pdf, /home/user/slides.pptx).

## When to use
- ANY time you need to read, analyze, summarize, or extract information from a document file — regardless of where it is located.
- This includes files downloaded with wget/curl to /tmp or any other path.
- NEVER use execute_bash (pdftotext, python3 -c "import pdfplumber...") or execute_ipython_cell to parse documents.
- NEVER install pdfplumber, PyPDF2, pymupdf, python-docx, python-pptx or similar libraries — use this tool instead.

## Arguments
- `file_path`: Absolute path to the document file anywhere in the sandbox.

file_path.description: Absolute path to the document file anywhere in the sandbox (e.g. /workspace/report.pdf, /tmp/downloaded.pdf, /home/user/slides.pptx).

function.name: task_tracker
function.description:
⚠️ CRITICAL: After task_tracker, ALWAYS add another tool_call in the SAME response! Never return task_tracker alone. ⚠️

Structured task list management:
- Create/update tasks with `plan` (todo/in_progress/done)
- View current tasks with `view`
- Keep one active task; remove obsolete items
- Each agent has its own isolated task list file

Examples of correct multi-action usage:
- [{"function": {"name": "task_tracker", ...}}, {"function": {"name": "run_ipython", "arguments": {"code": "..."}}}]
- [{"function": {"name": "task_tracker", ...}}, {"function": {"name": "str_replace_editor", "arguments": {"command": "view", "path": "..."}}}]
- [{"function": {"name": "task_tracker", ...}}, {"function": {"name": "execute_bash", "arguments": {"command": "pytest tests/"}}}]

Task tracking only records plans - you must act on them immediately.

command.description: The command to execute. `view` shows the current task list. `plan` creates or updates the task list based on provided requirements and progress.

function.name: microagent_trigger
function.description:
Microagent knowledge retrieval.

Microagents are prompt snippets loaded,that provide specialized guidance. This tool injects
the selected microagent knowledge into the context as extra information.
Provide `microagents`: a list of explicit microagent names to recall (preferred for full control).
If no knowledge matches, the tool response will indicate that nothing was found.

query.description: Text to run keyword matching when `microagents` is empty.

function.name: list_microagents
function.description:
List available microagents for this agent.
Returns the names of microagents that this agent can trigger.
After reviewing the list, choose and trigger relevant microagents yourself with `microagent_trigger`.
Do not ask the user to choose from the list unless a real ambiguity blocks progress.

function.name: query_ragflow
function.description:
Query a RAGFlow agent to generate retrieval-augmented responses.

## Usage Guidelines

- Provide a clear query in `query`.
- Avoid repeated queries for the same question. Answer after one query unless results are irrelevant.


## Error Handling and Troubleshooting
You MUST query the vector database using the tool whenever you encounter an error, warning, or unexpected behavior related to RTL design, verification, synthesis, or EDA tool usage. This includes, but is not limited to:
    - Simulation failures
    - Synthesis warnings/errors
    - Timing violations
    - Openlane flow failures
    - Verification failures
    - IP integration issues
    - Environment setup problems
    - Caravel integration issues
    - Caravel RTL issue
    - Wishbone bus integration issues
    - Bus protocol mismatches
    - Bus wrapper issues
    - Timing constraint issues
    - PnR issues
    - DRC/LVS issues
    - Design rule violations
    - Power Performance Area (PPA) issues
    - Issues with clock domain crossings (CDC)
    - Issues with reset architecture
    - Issues with asynchronous interfaces
    - Issues with testbenches
    - Issues with cocotb or pyuvm
    - Issues with using system verilog
    - Issues using Caravel-cocotb
    - Issues with using PRE_INSTALLED_IPS
    - Unexpected output or behavior from tools
    - Any other technical issues related to ASIC design, RTL, verification, synthesis, or EDA tools

query.description: The user query text to send to RAGFlow.

model.description: Optional model identifier. Defaults to RAGFLOW_MODEL.

extra_body.description: Optional extra RAGFlow parameters (e.g., reference settings).

function.name: str_replace_editor
function.description:
Custom editing tool for viewing, creating and editing files in plain-text format
* State is persistent across command calls and discussions with the user
* If `path` is a text file, `view` displays the result of applying `cat -n`. If `path` is a directory, `view` lists non-hidden files and directories up to 2 levels deep
* The following binary file extensions can be viewed in Markdown format: [".xlsx", ".pptx", ".wav", ".mp3", ".m4a", ".flac", ".pdf", ".docx"]. IT DOES NOT HANDLE IMAGES.
* The `create` command cannot be used if the specified `path` already exists as a file
* If a `command` generates a long output, it will be truncated and marked with `<response clipped>`
* The `undo_edit` command will revert the last edit made to the file at `path`
* This tool can be used for creating and editing files in plain-text format.


Before using this tool:
1. Use the view tool to understand the file's contents and context
2. Verify the directory path is correct (only applicable when creating new files):
   - Use the view tool to verify the parent directory exists and is the correct location

When making edits:
   - Ensure the edit results in idiomatic, correct code
   - Do not leave the code in a broken state
   - Always use absolute file paths (starting with /)

Always make sure the file is ended by a newline character. If you create a file, ensure it ends with exactly one newline character. If you edit a file, ensure the last line ends with a newline character.

CRITICAL REQUIREMENTS FOR USING THIS TOOL:

1. EXACT MATCHING: The `old_str` parameter must match EXACTLY one or more consecutive lines from the file, including all whitespace and indentation. The tool will fail if `old_str` matches multiple locations or doesn't match exactly with the file content.

2. UNIQUENESS: The `old_str` must uniquely identify a single instance in the file:
   - Include sufficient context before and after the change point (3-5 lines recommended)
   - If not unique, the replacement will not be performed

3. REPLACEMENT: The `new_str` parameter should contain the edited lines that replace the `old_str`. Both strings must be different.

Remember: when making multiple file edits in a row to the same file, you should prefer to send all edits in a single message with multiple calls to this tool, rather than multiple messages with a single call each.

command.description: The commands to run. Allowed options are: `view`, `create`, `str_replace`, `insert`, `undo_edit`.

path.description: Absolute path to file or directory, e.g. `/workspace/file.py` or `/workspace`.

file_text.description: Required parameter of `create` command, with the content of the file to be created.

old_str.description: Required parameter of `str_replace` command containing the string in `path` to replace.

new_str.description: Optional parameter of `str_replace` command containing the new string (if not given, no string will be added). Required parameter of `insert` command containing the string to insert.

insert_line.description: Required parameter of `insert` command. The `new_str` will be inserted AFTER the line `insert_line` of `path`.

view_range.description: Optional parameter of `view` command when `path` points to a file. If none is given, the full file is shown. If provided, the file will be shown in the indicated line number range, e.g. [11, 12] will show lines 11 and 12. Indexing at 1 to start. Setting `[start_line, -1]` shows all lines from `start_line` to the end of the file.

function.name: return_to_delegator
function.description:
Return control to the delegator agent with your results.

Use this tool when you have completed your delegated task and need to return control to the delegator.

CRITICAL REQUIREMENTS:
- This is the ONLY way to complete your delegated task
- You MUST use this tool instead of the 'finish' tool
- Include a clear summary of what you accomplished
- Include any results, outputs, or important information for the delegator
- If you encountered blockers or couldn't complete the task, explain why

The result should include:
- Status: "completed", "partial", or "failed"
- Summary of actions taken
- Key findings or outputs
- Any blockers or issues encountered
- Recommendations for next steps (if applicable)

status.description: The completion status of your delegated task

summary.description: A clear summary of what you accomplished and any important findings

outputs.description: Any structured data or results to return to the delegator

next_steps.description: Optional recommendations for what the delegator should do next

microagent_knowledge.name: knowledge/nc_chipgen
microagent_knowledge.content:
# nc_chipgen — SoC integration pipeline

**Scope:** End-to-end generator from **`config/soc.yaml`** (under `<project_root>`) to compute subsystem RTL, padframe, chip top, and OpenLane collateral. **Not** the soft-IP package flow (`hdl/`, `dv/`).

## Inputs / outputs

- **Primary config:** `<project_root>/config/soc.yaml` (SoC chipgen schema; align field semantics with **`knowledge/nc_socgen.md`** for the NC_SOCGEN portion).
- **Run from** `<project_root>` with `--out` pointing at the project root (or as orchestrator specifies).
- **Typical flags:** `--soc-config config/soc.yaml`, `--pdk-root`, `--nc-lib-root` (peripheral IP install), optional `--padframe-gen-root` / `--socgen-root` if not on PATH; synthesis: default Yosys, or `--skip-synth`, `--full-synth`, or `--core-width` / `--core-height` per spec.

## Eight steps (verify outputs after a clean run)

| Step | Check |
|------|--------|
| 1 NC_SOCGEN | `verilog/rtl/` compute SS (e.g. `compute_ss.v` / `<top_module>.v`), `tb/`, `fw/`, `filelists/<top>.f` |
| 2 Padframe config | Padframe handoff / `config/*padframe*.yaml` as produced |
| 3 Area estimate | `reports/synth_area.txt` or floorplan from `--full-synth` |
| 4 Update padframe | Padframe config reflects core dimensions |
| 5 padframe-gen | `gds/padframe.gds`, `verilog/rtl/padframe.v`, `def/padframe.def` |
| 6 Wrapper | `verilog/rtl/<design>_wrapper.v` |
| 7 Chip top | `verilog/rtl/<design>_chip.v` |
| 8 OpenLane | `openlane/<design>_wrapper/config.json`, `pdn_cfg.tcl`, `macro.cfg`, SDC |

## Authoring guardrails

- Set **`generation.emit_padframe_handoff: true`** in `soc.yaml` — required for the pipeline past step 1.
- Fix **`soc.yaml` and re-run** `nc_chipgen` when generated RTL or configs are wrong; do not hand-edit generated deliverables as the primary fix.

## Reproducibility

- Write **`<project_root>/manifest/tool_versions.txt`** with pinned `nc_chipgen`, `nc_socgen`, `padframe-gen`, simulator/synth tools, Python, and PDK note — see **`soc_config_gen`** prompt checklist for the minimum set.

microagent_knowledge.name: knowledge/softIP_verification
microagent_knowledge.content:
# Digital Verification Guide with Open-Source Tools

**ONLY REFER TO THIS IF YOU ARE VERIFYING A SOFT-IP**
**NEVER USE THIS TO VERIFY A CARAVEL INTEGRATION**

## Core Verification Principles
When performing digital verification tasks, follow these mandatory principles:
1. **Project root only:** Put all new tests, env code, logs, and coverage outputs under **`<project_root>`** (the cloned IP repo)—not arbitrary paths outside it.
2. **Always review Verilog/SystemVerilog files first** before proposing tests
3. **Write PyUVM/Cocotb tests by default**, not plain Verilog testbenches
4. **Always add timeout parameters** to prevent hanging tests (e.g., `@cocotb.test(timeout_time=10000, timeout_unit='us')`)
5. **Measure coverage continuously** and use it to improve verification
6. **RTL simulation with coverage is the sole default deliverable** for NativeChips **soft IP**. **Do not** plan or require **gate-level (netlist) simulation**—the SoC team synthesizes RTL in their environment; GLS is their responsibility unless the user **explicitly** contracts netlist-level verification and provides netlist/SDF. In the verification report, you may state **“GLS — out of scope (soft IP RTL deliverable; integrator synthesis path)”** when applicable.
7. **Commercial formal / FPV** (JasperGold, VC Formal, OneSpin, etc.) is **not** assumed available in the NativeChips CI environment—do not block soft-IP signoff on in-house FPV unless the user supplies external formal results.

### NativeChips coverage targets (soft IP)

- **Source (RTL):** Treat line/statement coverage as **incomplete until finished**: **target 100%** on in-scope RTL (merged across runs). **Always** document any remaining uncovered lines/toggles with a **named exclusion** (file/line, hierarchy, or pragma)—do not silently stop in the 90s.
- **Functional:** **Aim for 100%** of the **modeled** bins and crosses tied to the spec; add tests or constrained‑random stimulus until bins close. Matrix **`WAIVED`** / written exceptions are **only** for **user-approved** plan/requirements deferrals **or** **technical** exclusion records with specific references—**not** informal “we waived” or “accepted risk” (see `ip_verification` `<coverage_policy>`).

### IP class and verification obligations (all soft IPs)

Classify each IP from the **spec pin list** and document in `docs/ip_verification_plan.md`. Obligations are **not** optional “extras”—they match UVM-style boundaries (driver → DUT → monitor → scoreboard).

| Class | Typical interfaces | Mandatory beyond CSR-only polling |
|--------|-------------------|----------------------------------|
| **A — MMIO / control‑centric** | APB/AHB-lite slave only; no off-chip protocol pins | Strong CSR/RAL tests, interrupts, FIFO/DMA as connected; scoreboard or equivalent if the IP transforms data |
| **B — Wire / serial / bus** | I3C, I2C, SPI, UART, etc. | **BFM** (or reactive peer) + **monitor** on those pins + **end-to-end** checks vs FIFO/CSR |
| **C — Streaming / packet** | AXI-Stream, credit/valid, custom datapath | Generator + monitor + scoreboard; backpressure per spec |
| **D — Mixed** | e.g. APB + protocol pins + IRQ + DMA | Union of obligations; plan lists which agent covers which boundary |

**Rule:** “No external memory/crypto ports” does **not** mean Class A only—protocol or stream pins require Class B/C TB content.

### Evidence-based triage (before blaming “bad tests” or “wrong simulator”)

When metrics look wrong (e.g. high functional %, low line %, or “tests pass but don’t check”), **split hypotheses**:

1. **Reproduce** merged coverage from **`make verify`** (or documented equivalent)—same hierarchy scope as the report.
2. Compare **per-test** vs **merged** line reports; confirm RTL-only scope if line % is questioned.
3. Review **full** test + env (checks may live in base classes/VIP).
4. List **uncovered lines** or document **exclusions** (file/line + reason).

Low line % does **not** always imply hollow tests; hollow tests do **not** always imply missing BFM—triage first.

### Default regression + coverage HTML (soft IP template)

Use **`nc_softip_project_template`**: **`make verify`** runs regression, merges Verilator `coverage.dat`, and writes **`dv/sim/coverage_html/`**. Pin **Verilator / cocotb / Python** in **`manifest/tool_versions.txt`** and README. The required `Makefile`, `tool_versions.txt`, and optional `verify.sh` templates are defined inline in that knowledge file.

## PyUVM Testbench Template

### Basic Test Structure
```python
@cocotb.test(timeout_time=10000, timeout_unit='us')
async def test_name(dut):
    """Always include timeout in decorator"""

    # 1. Setup logging
    logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

    # 2. Configure UVM
    uvm_config_db.set(None, "*", "DUT", dut)

    # 3. Start clock
    clock = Clock(dut.clk, 10, units="ns")
    cocotb.start_soon(clock.start())

    # 4. Reset
    dut.rst_n.value = 0
    await ClockCycles(dut.clk, 5)
    dut.rst_n.value = 1
    await ClockCycles(dut.clk, 5)

    # 5. Run UVM test
    await uvm_root().run_test("TestClassName")
```

### Transaction with Constraints (PyVSC)
```python
@pyvsc.randobj
class Transaction(uvm_sequence_item):
    def __init__(self, name="Transaction"):
        super().__init__(name)
        self.addr = pyvsc.rand_uint32_t()
        self.data = pyvsc.rand_uint32_t()
        self.cmd = pyvsc.rand_enum_t('READ', 'WRITE')

    @pyvsc.constraint
    def valid_addr_c(self):
        self.addr >= 0x1000
        self.addr <= 0xFFFF

    @pyvsc.constraint
    def alignment_c(self):
        (self.addr & 0x3) == 0  # 4-byte aligned
```

### Driver Template
```python
class Driver(uvm_driver):
    def __init__(self, name, parent):
        super().__init__(name, parent)

    def build_phase(self):
        self.dut = uvm_config_db.get(self, "", "DUT")

    async def run_phase(self):
        while True:
            item = await self.seq_item_port.get_next_item()
            await self.drive_item(item)
            self.seq_item_port.item_done()

    async def drive_item(self, item):
        # Add timeout protection
        timeout_cycles = 0
        self.dut.addr.value = item.addr
        self.dut.data.value = item.data
        self.dut.valid.value = 1

        while not self.dut.ready.value:
            await RisingEdge(self.dut.clk)
            timeout_cycles += 1
            if timeout_cycles > 1000:
                raise TimeoutError("Driver timeout")

        self.dut.valid.value = 0
        await RisingEdge(self.dut.clk)
```

### Monitor Template

```python
class Monitor(uvm_monitor):
    def __init__(self, name, parent):
        super().__init__(name, parent)
        self.analysis_port = uvm_analysis_port("analysis_port", self)

    def build_phase(self):
        self.dut = uvm_config_db.get(self, "", "DUT")

    async def run_phase(self):
        while True:
            await RisingEdge(self.dut.clk)
            if self.dut.valid.value and self.dut.ready.value:
                trans = Transaction("monitored")
                trans.addr = int(self.dut.addr.value)
                trans.data = int(self.dut.data.value)
                self.analysis_port.write(trans)
```

## Functional Coverage with PyVSC

### Coverage Model Template
```python
@pyvsc.covergroup
class FunctionalCoverage:
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()

        # Coverpoints
        self.cp_cmd = pyvsc.coverpoint(
            lambda: self.cmd,
            bins={
                "read": pyvsc.bin_array([], ['READ']),
                "write": pyvsc.bin_array([], ['WRITE'])
            }
        )

        self.cp_addr = pyvsc.coverpoint(
            lambda: self.addr,
            bins={
                "low": pyvsc.bin_array([pyvsc.rng(0x0000, 0x7FFF)]),
                "high": pyvsc.bin_array([pyvsc.rng(0x8000, 0xFFFF)])
            }
        )

        # Cross coverage
        self.cross_cmd_addr = pyvsc.cross([self.cp_cmd, self.cp_addr])

    def sample(self, trans):
        self.cmd = trans.cmd
        self.addr = trans.addr
        super().sample()
```

### Coverage Collector Template
```python
class CoverageCollector(uvm_subscriber):
    def __init__(self, name, parent):
        super().__init__(name, parent)
        self.coverage = FunctionalCoverage()

    def write(self, trans):
        self.coverage.sample(trans)

    def report_phase(self):
        cov_percent = self.coverage.get_coverage_percentage()
        self.logger.info(f"Functional Coverage: {cov_percent:.2f}%")

        if cov_percent < 95:
            self.logger.warning("Coverage goal not met (95% required)")
```

### Coverage Analysis in Python
```python
# verification/coverage/analyze_verilator_coverage.py
import subprocess
import re

def analyze_verilator_coverage(coverage_file="coverage.dat"):
    """Analyze Verilator coverage and return metrics"""

    # Get coverage summary
    result = subprocess.run(
        ['verilator_coverage', coverage_file],
        capture_output=True, text=True
    )

    metrics = {}
    for line in result.stdout.splitlines():
        # Parse line coverage
        if 'Lines Covered' in line:
            match = re.search(r'(\d+\.\d+)%', line)
            if match:
                metrics['line_coverage'] = float(match.group(1))

        # Parse toggle coverage
        elif 'Toggle Covered' in line:
            match = re.search(r'(\d+\.\d+)%', line)
            if match:
                metrics['toggle_coverage'] = float(match.group(1))

    return metrics

def check_coverage_goals(coverage_file, line_goal=90, toggle_goal=85):
    """Check if coverage goals are met"""
    metrics = analyze_verilator_coverage(coverage_file)

    passed = True
    if metrics.get('line_coverage', 0) < line_goal:
        print(f"FAIL: Line coverage {metrics['line_coverage']}% < {line_goal}%")
        passed = False

    if metrics.get('toggle_coverage', 0) < toggle_goal:
        print(f"FAIL: Toggle coverage {metrics['toggle_coverage']}% < {toggle_goal}%")
        passed = False

    return passed
```

### Coverage Exclusions in Verilog
```verilog
// In Verilog/SystemVerilog source files

module example (
    input clk,
    input rst,
    input [7:0] data,
    output reg [7:0] result
);

    // Normal covered code
    always @(posedge clk) begin
        if (rst) begin
            result <= 8'b0;
        end else begin
            result <= data + 1;
        end
    end

    // Exclude from coverage
    // verilator coverage_off
    initial begin
        $display("Debug message");  // Not covered
    end
    // verilator coverage_on

    // Exclude specific line
    always @(posedge clk) begin
        // verilator coverage_off_line
        $display("Debug: data=%h", data);  // This line not covered

        result <= data;  // This is covered
    end

endmodule
```

### Python Test Runner
```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import os
from pathlib import Path
from cocotb.runner import get_runner

def run_test_with_coverage(top_module, test_module, simulator='verilator'):
    """Run test with coverage enabled"""

    runner = get_runner(simulator)
    proj_path = Path(__file__).parent.parent

    # Collect sources
    sources = list(proj_path.glob("rtl/**/*.v"))
    sources.extend(proj_path.glob("rtl/**/*.sv"))

    # Build arguments
    build_args = []

    if simulator == 'verilator':
        build_args = [
            '--coverage', '--coverage-line', '--coverage-toggle',
            '--coverage-user', '--coverage-underscore',
            '--trace', '--trace-structs',
            '-Wall', '-Wno-DECLFILENAME',
            '-O3', '--threads', str(os.cpu_count())
        ]
    elif simulator == 'icarus':
        build_args = ['-g2012', '-Wall']

    # Build
    runner.build(
        sources=[str(s) for s in sources],
        hdl_toplevel=top_module,
        build_args=build_args,
        waves=True
    )

    # Run test
    runner.test(
        hdl_toplevel=top_module,
        test_module=test_module
    )

    # Analyze coverage if Verilator
    if simulator == 'verilator':
        analyze_coverage()

def analyze_coverage():
    """Post-process coverage data"""
    import subprocess

    # Generate annotated source
    subprocess.run([
        'verilator_coverage',
        '--annotate', 'coverage_annotated',
        'sim_build/coverage.dat'
    ])

    # Generate summary
    result = subprocess.run([
        'verilator_coverage',
        'sim_build/coverage.dat'
    ], capture_output=True, text=True)

    print("\nCOVERAGE SUMMARY:")
    print(result.stdout)

    # Check goals
    import re
    for line in result.stdout.splitlines():
        if 'Lines' in line:
            match = re.search(r'(\d+\.\d+)%', line)
            if match:
                cov = float(match.group(1))
                if cov < 90:
                    print(f"WARNING: Line coverage {cov}% is below 90% goal")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    run_test_with_coverage('alu', 'test_alu')
```

## Critical Reminders for Test Writing

### Always Include Timeouts
```python
# WRONG - Can hang forever
@cocotb.test()
async def bad_test(dut):
    while not dut.done.value:
        await RisingEdge(dut.clk)

# CORRECT - Has timeout protection
@cocotb.test(timeout_time=10000, timeout_unit='us')
async def good_test(dut):
    timeout = 0
    while not dut.done.value:
        await RisingEdge(dut.clk)
        timeout += 1
        if timeout > 1000:
            raise TimeoutError("Timeout waiting for done")
```

### Always Check Randomization
```python
# WRONG
trans = Transaction()
trans.randomize()  # Might fail silently

# CORRECT
trans = Transaction()
assert trans.randomize(), "Randomization failed"
```

### Proper Signal Sampling
```python
# WRONG - Race condition
await RisingEdge(dut.clk)
value = dut.signal.value  # Might catch transition

# CORRECT - Sample after settling
await RisingEdge(dut.clk)
await Timer(1, units='ns')  # Let signals settle
value = dut.signal.value
```

microagent_knowledge.name: knowledge/standalone_soc
microagent_knowledge.content:
# Standalone SoC Design — No Caravel

## Overview
A complete SoC design flow that is entirely independent of Caravel. Uses NC_SOCGEN to generate the compute subsystem and padframe-gen to generate the IO ring. The result is a full chip: `soc_top` = `compute_ss` + `padframe` + clock/reset + analog blocks.

## Architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   soc_top                        │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐│
│  │              padframe                        ││
│  │  (IO pads, power pads, corners, spacers)    ││
│  │                                              ││
│  │  ┌───────────────────────────────────────┐  ││
│  │  │           core area                    │  ││
│  │  │                                        │  ││
│  │  │  ┌─────────────────────────────┐      │  ││
│  │  │  │      compute_ss             │      │  ││
│  │  │  │  (CPU + AHB + APB + periphs)│      │  ││
│  │  │  └─────────────────────────────┘      │  ││
│  │  │                                        │  ││
│  │  │  ┌──────────┐  ┌───────────────┐      │  ││
│  │  │  │MS_CLK_RST│  │ Analog blocks │      │  ││
│  │  │  └──────────┘  └───────────────┘      │  ││
│  │  └───────────────────────────────────────┘  ││
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘│
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**No Caravel dependency**: No Wishbone at top level, no mprj_io, no user_project_wrapper, no management SoC. The standalone SoC owns its entire address space, clock tree, reset, and IO.

## Internal Bus Architecture
- **AHB-Lite** inside compute_ss (generated by NC_SOCGEN)
- **APB bridges** to peripherals (up to 2 buses × 16 slots)
- **APB passthrough** ports for clk_rst_pwr and io_cfg_ss (modules sit OUTSIDE compute_ss)

## SRAM
- SRAM **must always use the CF_SRAM compiled macro** (e.g., `CF_SRAM_1024x32`). Never synthesize memory from registers or flip-flops.
- The `nc_ahb_to_sram` module is the AHB-Lite bridge that wraps the CF_SRAM macro.
- `CF_SRAM` must always be included in `link_IPs.json`.

## Project Directory Structure

Created by the `soc_setup` agent at the start of the flow. IPs are linked via `ipm_linker`.

```
<proj_root>/
├── docs/
│   ├── requirements.md          # SoC requirements
│   ├── soc_config.yaml          # NC_SOCGEN input config
│   ├── padframe_config.yaml     # padframe-gen input config (initial draft)
│   ├── register_map.md          # Peripheral register documentation
│   ├── pad_map.md               # Pin-to-function mapping
│   ├── integration_notes.md     # Integration constraints
│   ├── soc_architecture.md      # Architecture documentation
│   ├── dv/
│   │   ├── verification_checklist.md   # Test plan (evaluator-owned)
│   │   ├── verification_checklist.html # HTML version
│   │   ├── verification_dashboard.html # Results dashboard

    ├── compute_ss/              # OpenLane config for macro hardening
    └── soc_top/                 # OpenLane config for top-level PnR
```

## Integration Rules (soc_top.v)

### Signal Mapping
The soc_top module wires compute_ss ports to padframe core-side ports:

1. **GPIO signals**: compute_ss `gpioa_out[i]` → padframe `core_gpio_i_out`, compute_ss `gpioa_oe[i]` → padframe `core_gpio_i_oe_n` (ACTIVE LOW inversion needed), padframe `core_gpio_i_in` → compute_ss `gpioa_in[i]`

2. **UART signals**: If UART is pinmuxed through GPIO (via nc_gpio AF matrix), no separate padframe mapping needed — it goes through GPIO pads. If direct: uart_tx → padframe GPIO out, padframe GPIO in → uart_rx.

3. **QSPI flash signals**: compute_ss `qspi_*` → dedicated padframe GPIO pads (or direct connection to flash)

4. **Reset**: padframe `core_reset_n_xres_h_n` → reset synchronizer → compute_ss `reset_n`

5. **Clock**: padframe `core_clk_in` → PLL/oscillator or direct → compute_ss `clk`

6. **APB passthrough ports**: compute_ss `clk_rst_pwr_p*` and `io_cfg_ss_p*` → external APB modules (MS_CLK_RST, IO_CFG_SS)

### Padframe GPIO Configuration
For each GPIO pad in the padframe, the core-side signals must be configured:
```verilog
// Example: Configure pad as output (push-pull, strong drive)
assign core_gpio_0_out = compute_ss_gpioa_out[0];
assign core_gpio_0_oe_n = ~compute_ss_gpioa_oe[0];  // invert: compute_ss is active-high, pad is active-low
assign core_gpio_0_dm_0 = 1'b0;  // drive mode: strong
assign core_gpio_0_dm_1 = 1'b1;
assign core_gpio_0_dm_2 = 1'b1;
assign core_gpio_0_inp_dis = 1'b0;
assign core_gpio_0_enable_h = 1'b1;
assign core_gpio_0_enable_vddio = 1'b1;
assign core_gpio_0_hld_h_n = 1'b1;  // not held
assign core_gpio_0_hld_ovr = 1'b0;
assign core_gpio_0_analog_en = 1'b0;
assign core_gpio_0_analog_sel = 1'b0;
assign core_gpio_0_analog_pol = 1'b0;
assign core_gpio_0_enable_inp_h = 1'b0;
assign core_gpio_0_enable_vdda_h = 1'b0;
assign core_gpio_0_enable_vswitch_h = 1'b0;
assign core_gpio_0_ib_mode_sel = 1'b0;
assign core_gpio_0_vtrip_sel = 1'b0;
assign core_gpio_0_slow = 1'b0;
// Read: gpioa_in[0] = core_gpio_0_in;
```

### soc_top is STRUCTURAL
- Primarily wire connections and signal inversions
- Minimal glue logic (only for clock/reset domain crossing)
- All processing happens inside compute_ss or dedicated modules
- No behavioral code in soc_top

## Power Domains
- **VCCD/VSSD** — Core 1.8V digital
- **VDDIO/VSSIO** — IO 3.3V
- **VDDA/VSSA** — Analog (optional, for mixed-signal designs)

## Clock and Reset
- External crystal/oscillator → padframe clock pad → MS_CLK_RST (PLL/divider) → compute_ss `clk`
- External reset → padframe reset pad → reset synchronizer → compute_ss `reset_n`
- MS_CLK_RST connected via APB passthrough from compute_ss

## Verification Strategy
Two-tier approach:

### Tier 1: NC_SOCGEN BFM Tests (Icarus Verilog)
Pre-generated Verilog testbenches that test the compute_ss in isolation:
- Smoke: register read/write for all peripherals
- IRQ: stimulus and acknowledgment
- DMA: transfer sequences
- XiP: flash read
- Pinmux: AF activation
- FW boot: firmware boot from XiP hex image

Run with: `iverilog -g2005 -f <top>.f -o sim tb/tb_<top>_smoke_bfm.v && vvp sim`
(Check `reports/<top>_compatibility.json` for correct Icarus flags)

### Tier 2: Cocotb Integration Tests
Python-driven tests for soc_top integration:
- Padframe connectivity
- Clock/reset startup sequence
- Multi-peripheral interactions
- Full system boot

## PnR Strategy (Hierarchical)
1. **Harden compute_ss** as a macro (OpenLane/LibreLane)
   - Inputs: compute_ss RTL, filelist, SDC constraints
   - Outputs: LEF, GDS, LIB for compute_ss
2. **Top-level PnR** with padframe as chip boundary
   - `def/core.def` as FP_DEF_TEMPLATE (defines die area, FENCE region, core-side pin definitions)
   - NOTE: Use `def/core.def`, NOT `def/padframe.def` for FP_DEF_TEMPLATE
   - compute_ss as pre-hardened macro placed inside core area (FENCE region)
   - Route compute_ss ports to padframe core-side ports
   - Power grid: core ring inside padframe, straps from power pads
3. **GDS merge**: `gds/padframe.gds` + compute_ss GDS + top routing

## Key Differences from Caravel Flow

| Aspect | Caravel Flow | Standalone SoC Flow |
|--------|-------------|-------------------|
| Top-level bus | Wishbone | AHB-Lite (internal to compute_ss) |
| IO | mprj_io[37:0] | Custom padframe (padframe-gen) |
| Wrapper | user_project_wrapper | soc_top.v |
| Die area | Fixed (~3000×3600µm) | Variable (from padframe-gen) |
| Management SoC | Included | None (standalone) |
| PnR boundary | Caravel harness | Padframe DEF |
| Clock source | Caravel clock | External oscillator + MS_CLK_RST |
| Reset source | Caravel reset | External reset pad |
| Address space | Shared with mgmt SoC | Full ownership |
| Peripheral bus | Wishbone slave | APB via AHB-Lite bridge |

## Handoff Between Stages

0. **Requirements → Setup**: IP list from `docs/requirements.md` and `docs/soc_config.yaml`
1. **Setup → SoC Gen**: Project directory created, IPs linked in `ip/`
2. **Requirements → SoC Gen**: `docs/soc_config.yaml` (NC_SOCGEN format)
3. **SoC Gen → Padframe Gen**: `rtl/compute_ss/reports/<top>_padframe_handoff.yaml`
4. **Requirements → Padframe Gen**: `docs/padframe_config.yaml` (initial pad/power requirements)
5. **Padframe Gen → Integration**: `verilog/padframe.v` (port names), `def/core.def` (core area)
6. **SoC Gen → Integration**: `rtl/compute_ss/rtl/<top>.v` (module ports), `rtl/compute_ss/<top>.f` (filelist)
7. **Integration → Verification**: `rtl/soc_top.v`, `rtl/soc_top.f`, `ip/` (firmware headers + dv examples)
8. **Integration → PnR**: `rtl/soc_top.f`, `def/core.def` (FP_DEF_TEMPLATE), `gds/padframe.gds` (for GDS merge)

# Requirements Document — MSPM33C321A Derivative MCU

**Design Name:** `mspm33c321a_derivative`
**Working Name:** `mspm33c321a_deriv`
**Date:** 2026-05-29
**Source:** `docs/user_brief.md`

---

## 1. Requirement Classification Legend

| Tag | Meaning |
|-----|---------|
| **EXPLICIT** | Stated verbatim in the user brief |
| **INFERRED** | Derived from reference device scope statement ("all other peripherals remain consistent") |
| **DEFAULT** | Applied by NC toolchain mapping rules; documented with rationale |

---

## 2. Explicit User Requirements

| # | Parameter | Reference (MSPM33C321A) | Derivative Target | Tag |
|---|-----------|------------------------|-------------------|-----|
| R-001 | CPU Core | ARM Cortex-M0+ | ARM Cortex-M0+ (unchanged) | EXPLICIT |
| R-002 | CPU Clock | 80 MHz max | 80 MHz max (unchanged) | EXPLICIT |
| R-003 | Flash (NVM) | 1 MB | **512 KB** | EXPLICIT |
| R-004 | SRAM | 64 KB (ref) | **128 KB** (upgraded) | EXPLICIT |
| R-005 | ADC Instances | 2 × 12-bit SAR | **1 × 12-bit SAR** (one removed) | EXPLICIT |
| R-006 | I2C Instances | 2 | **3** (one added) | EXPLICIT |
| R-007 | Design Name | — | `mspm33c321a_derivative` | EXPLICIT |
| R-008 | Scope | — | Cost-reduced derivative; all other subsystems unchanged | EXPLICIT |

---

## 3. Inferred Requirements (from Reference Device Scope)

The user brief states: *"All other peripherals, clocking, pinout strategy, and subsystem architecture remain consistent with the reference device."* The following are inferred from the MSPM33C321A datasheet profile:

| # | Parameter | Value | Rationale |
|---|-----------|-------|-----------|
| R-009 | UART Instances | 4 | MSPM33C321A reference: 4 UART/IrDA modules |
| R-010 | SPI Instances | 2 | MSPM33C321A reference: 2 SPI masters/slaves |
| R-011 | General-Purpose Timers | 4 (16-bit) | MSPM33C321A reference: 4 GP 16-bit timers |
| R-012 | Advanced Timers (PWM) | 2 | MSPM33C321A reference: 2 advanced control timers for PWM |
| R-013 | DMA Channels | 8 channels (1 controller) | MSPM33C321A reference: 8-channel DMA |
| R-014 | CRC Accelerator | 1 | MSPM33C321A reference: hardware CRC |
| R-015 | AES Accelerator | 1 (AES-128/256) | MSPM33C321A reference: hardware AES engine |
| R-016 | Windowed Watchdog | 1 (WWDT) | MSPM33C321A reference: WWDT |
| R-017 | Real-Time Clock | 1 (RTC) | MSPM33C321A reference: RTC with calendar |
| R-018 | GPIO | 3 ports × 16 pins = 48 GPIO | MSPM33C321A reference: multi-port GPIO; 3 ports cover package pin budget |
| R-019 | Package | LQFP-64 (primary) | Most common package for MSPM33C321A family; derivative maintains pinout compatibility |
| R-020 | SWD Debug | Yes (2-pin SWD) | MSPM33C321A uses ARM SWD; derivative preserves debug access |
| R-021 | XIP (Execute-in-Place) | Yes | Internal flash requires XIP controller for code execution |
| R-022 | Interrupt Controller | Built-in to CPU | M0+ has NVIC; maps to hazard3 internal PLIC in NC framework |

---

## 4. Defaults Applied (NC Toolchain Mapping)

| # | Parameter | Applied Default | Rationale |
|---|-----------|----------------|-----------|
| D-001 | CPU Implementation | `hazard3_2port_swd` (RISC-V RV32I) | NC framework supports RISC-V only. Hazard3 is the functional equivalent of ARM Cortex-M0+: same pipeline depth, SWD debug, NVIC-equivalent PLIC. Provides identical embedded SW programming model. |
| D-002 | Interrupt Policy | `hazard3_internal` | Hazard3 has a built-in PLIC; external `nc_pic` not used with hazard3 CPU type. |
| D-003 | Bus Architecture | AHB-Lite + APB | Standard NC_SOCGEN fabric; matches M0+ typical bus topology. |
| D-004 | APB Buses | 2 buses × 16 slots | 26 peripheral APB slots required; 2 buses of 16 = 32 slots available (26 used). |
| D-005 | XIP Base Address | `0x00000000` | NC standard: flash/XIP mapped at base of address space for direct boot execution. |
| D-006 | SRAM Base Address | `0x20000000` | ARM-conventional SRAM base address preserved for toolchain / linker compatibility. |
| D-007 | APB Base Address | `0x40000000` | ARM-conventional peripheral base; matches M0+ ecosystem linker/HAL expectations. |
| D-008 | SRAM Module | `nc_ahb_to_sram` + `CF_SRAM_1024x32` | NC mandates compiled SRAM macros, never register-based memory. |
| D-009 | ADC Treatment | Noted in spec; not in digital RTL | NC framework does not include a synthesizable ADC. ADC requirement (1 × 12-bit SAR) is documented for integration at mixed-signal level. |
| D-010 | PWM | `nc_pwm` × 2 | Maps MSPM33C321A advanced timer PWM capability to NC PWM peripheral. |
| D-011 | GPIO Reset Direction | Input | Safe power-on default; all pads high-impedance at reset. |

---

## 5. IPs Required

| IP Name | Type | Instances | Notes |
|---------|------|-----------|-------|
| NC_RV32 | CPU | 1 | Hazard3 RV32I core (M0+ equivalent) |
| NC_AMBA | Bus fabric | 1 | AHB-Lite + APB bridge infrastructure |
| NC_COMMON | Utilities | 1 | Clock cells, resets, glue logic |
| CF_SRAM_1024x32 | SRAM macro | 4 | 4 × 32KB = 128KB (4 banks of 1K×32-bit) |
| NC_XIP | Flash controller | 1 | 512KB execute-in-place flash interface |
| NC_GPIO | GPIO | 3 | GPIOA, GPIOB, GPIOC (16 pins each) |
| NC_UART | UART | 4 | UART0–UART3 |
| NC_SPI | SPI | 2 | SPI0–SPI1 |
| NC_I2C | I2C | 3 | I2C0–I2C2 (reference 2 + derivative +1) |
| NC_TMR | General Timer | 6 | TMR0–TMR5 (4 GP + 2 advanced) |
| NC_PWM | PWM | 2 | PWM0–PWM1 |
| NC_DMAC | DMA Controller | 1 | 8-channel DMA |
| NC_CRC | CRC Accelerator | 1 | Hardware CRC |
| NC_AES | AES Accelerator | 1 | AES-128/256 |
| NC_WWDT | Watchdog | 1 | Windowed watchdog timer |
| NC_RTC | RTC | 1 | Real-time clock with calendar |
| NC_SYSTICK | SysTick | 1 | ARM-compatible SysTick timer |

---

## 6. Requirements NOT in Scope

The following MSPM33C321A features are noted as out-of-scope for this derivative:

| Feature | Status | Note |
|---------|--------|------|
| ADC (2nd instance) | **Removed** per brief | Only 1 ADC remains; handled at mixed-signal level |
| MCAN/CAN FD | Not carried forward | NC framework has no NC_CAN; excluded pending future derivative |
| TRNG | Not carried forward | NC framework has no NC_TRNG; noted for future addition |
| VREF / Analog comparator | Not in digital RTL | Analog-only block; outside NC digital framework |
| Bootloader ROM | NC default | NC_SOCGEN XIP + startup covers boot; no separate ROM block |

---

## 7. Verification Expectations

| Item | Expected Coverage |
|------|------------------|
| All APB peripherals | Register smoke tests (read/write) |
| UART, SPI, I2C, GPIO | Functional directed tests (loopback / self-check) |
| DMA transfers | Memory-to-memory + peripheral transfer tests |
| Timer / PWM | Period/duty-cycle configuration tests |
| AES / CRC | Known-answer tests (KAT) |
| WWDT | Timeout and windowed service tests |
| IRQ handling | All peripheral IRQ assertion and acknowledgment |
| XIP flash | Boot from hex image via XiP BFM |
| SWD debug | Debug access port connection test |

# Device Specification — MSPM33C321A Derivative MCU

**Document ID:** SPEC-MSPM33C321A-DERIV-001
**Design Name:** `mspm33c321a_derivative`
**Version:** 1.0-draft
**Date:** 2026-05-29
**Status:** Draft — Stage 1 Requirements Output

---

## 1. Device Overview

The `mspm33c321a_derivative` is a cost-optimised derivative of the Texas Instruments MSPM33C321A microcontroller. It targets the same application space (industrial sensing, motor control, IoT edge nodes) while reducing non-volatile memory from 1 MB to 512 KB, increasing SRAM from 64 KB to 128 KB, removing one 12-bit ADC instance, and adding one I2C interface. All other subsystems, peripheral topology, clocking, and pinout strategy are carried forward from the reference device.

In the NC silicon toolchain the device is realised using a **RISC-V Hazard3** processor core (a functional Cortex-M0+ equivalent with SWD debug), the NC_SOCGEN AHB-Lite/APB fabric, and NC-qualified IP peripherals.

---

## 2. Key Specifications Summary

| Parameter | Specification |
|-----------|--------------|
| **CPU Core** | RISC-V Hazard3 RV32I (Cortex-M0+ functional equivalent) |
| **Debug** | 2-pin SWD |
| **CPU Clock** | Up to 80 MHz |
| **Program Memory** | 512 KB internal Flash (XIP) |
| **Data Memory** | 128 KB SRAM (4 × 32 KB compiled macro banks) |
| **Bus Architecture** | AHB-Lite (core bus) + 2 × APB bridge (peripheral bus) |
| **Package** | LQFP-64 (primary), VQFN-64 (alternate) |
| **GPIO** | 48 pins — GPIOA[15:0], GPIOB[15:0], GPIOC[15:0] |
| **UART** | 4 instances (UART0–UART3) |
| **SPI** | 2 instances (SPI0–SPI1) |
| **I2C** | 3 instances (I2C0–I2C2) ← +1 vs reference |
| **Timers** | 6 instances: 4 × GP 16-bit (TMR0–TMR3) + 2 × advanced (TMR4–TMR5) |
| **PWM** | 2 instances (PWM0–PWM1, derived from advanced timers) |
| **DMA** | 1 controller, 8 channels |
| **ADC** | 1 × 12-bit SAR ← −1 vs reference (mixed-signal block) |
| **AES** | 1 instance (AES-128/256) |
| **CRC** | 1 instance |
| **WWDT** | 1 windowed watchdog |
| **RTC** | 1 real-time clock with calendar |
| **SysTick** | 1 ARM-compatible SysTick timer |
| **Supply Voltage** | 1.8 V core (VCCD), 3.3 V IO (VDDIO) |
| **Temp Range** | −40 °C to +125 °C (industrial) |

---

## 3. Memory Map

| Region | Base Address | Size | Description |
|--------|-------------|------|-------------|
| Flash (XIP) | `0x0000_0000` | 512 KB (`0x0008_0000`) | Execute-in-place program flash |
| Reserved | `0x0008_0000` | — | Flash address space upper boundary |
| SRAM | `0x2000_0000` | 128 KB (`0x0002_0000`) | Data / stack / heap SRAM |
| APB Bus 0 | `0x4000_0000` | 64 KB (`0x0001_0000`) | Peripheral slots 0–15 (16 × 4 KB) |
| APB Bus 1 | `0x4001_0000` | 64 KB (`0x0001_0000`) | Peripheral slots 16–31 (16 × 4 KB) |
| Peripheral Region | `0x4000_0000` | — | All APB peripherals |

### 3.1 Peripheral Address Allocation (APB — illustrative, final offsets from NC_SOCGEN)

| Peripheral | Instance | APB Bus | APB Slot | Base Address (est.) |
|------------|----------|---------|----------|---------------------|
| GPIO | GPIOA | 0 | 0 | `0x4000_0000` |
| GPIO | GPIOB | 0 | 1 | `0x4000_1000` |
| GPIO | GPIOC | 0 | 2 | `0x4000_2000` |
| UART | UART0 | 0 | 3 | `0x4000_3000` |
| UART | UART1 | 0 | 4 | `0x4000_4000` |
| UART | UART2 | 0 | 5 | `0x4000_5000` |
| UART | UART3 | 0 | 6 | `0x4000_6000` |
| SPI | SPI0 | 0 | 7 | `0x4000_7000` |
| SPI | SPI1 | 0 | 8 | `0x4000_8000` |
| I2C | I2C0 | 0 | 9 | `0x4000_9000` |
| I2C | I2C1 | 0 | 10 | `0x4000_A000` |
| I2C | I2C2 | 0 | 11 | `0x4000_B000` |
| TMR | TMR0 | 0 | 12 | `0x4000_C000` |
| TMR | TMR1 | 0 | 13 | `0x4000_D000` |
| TMR | TMR2 | 0 | 14 | `0x4000_E000` |
| TMR | TMR3 | 0 | 15 | `0x4000_F000` |
| TMR | TMR4 | 1 | 0 | `0x4001_0000` |
| TMR | TMR5 | 1 | 1 | `0x4001_1000` |
| PWM | PWM0 | 1 | 2 | `0x4001_2000` |
| PWM | PWM1 | 1 | 3 | `0x4001_3000` |
| DMAC | DMAC0 | 1 | 4 | `0x4001_4000` |
| CRC | CRC0 | 1 | 5 | `0x4001_5000` |
| AES | AES0 | 1 | 6 | `0x4001_6000` |
| WWDT | WWDT0 | 1 | 7 | `0x4001_7000` |
| RTC | RTC0 | 1 | 8 | `0x4001_8000` |
| SysTick | SYSTICK0 | 1 | 9 | `0x4001_9000` |

> **Note:** Final base addresses are assigned by NC_SOCGEN during RTL generation; the table above is illustrative.

---

## 4. Interrupt Map (Estimated)

NC_SOCGEN auto-assigns interrupt vectors starting at IRQ0. The Hazard3 internal PLIC handles all peripheral interrupts. Below is the estimated IRQ assignment order:

| IRQ | Peripheral | Source |
|-----|-----------|--------|
| 0 | GPIOA | GPIO port A combined |
| 1 | GPIOB | GPIO port B combined |
| 2 | GPIOC | GPIO port C combined |
| 3 | UART0 | TX/RX/error |
| 4 | UART1 | TX/RX/error |
| 5 | UART2 | TX/RX/error |
| 6 | UART3 | TX/RX/error |
| 7 | SPI0 | TX/RX/done |
| 8 | SPI1 | TX/RX/done |
| 9 | I2C0 | Transfer complete/error |
| 10 | I2C1 | Transfer complete/error |
| 11 | I2C2 | Transfer complete/error (new instance) |
| 12 | TMR0 | Overflow/compare |
| 13 | TMR1 | Overflow/compare |
| 14 | TMR2 | Overflow/compare |
| 15 | TMR3 | Overflow/compare |
| 16 | TMR4 | Overflow/compare |
| 17 | TMR5 | Overflow/compare |
| 18 | PWM0 | Fault/period |
| 19 | PWM1 | Fault/period |
| 20 | DMAC0 | Channel done/error |
| 21 | CRC0 | Done |
| 22 | AES0 | Done |
| 23 | WWDT0 | Reset-warning / window-violation |
| 24 | RTC0 | Alarm/periodic |
| 25 | SYSTICK0 | SysTick underflow |

> **Derivative delta:** IRQ 11 (I2C2) is new vs. reference. ADC1 IRQ (previously assigned) is freed.

---

## 5. GPIO and Pinmux

### 5.1 GPIO Ports

| Port | Width | Reset Direction | Alternate Functions |
|------|-------|----------------|---------------------|
| GPIOA | 16 | Input (Hi-Z) | UART0/1 TX/RX, SPI0, I2C0/1, TMR0/1 |
| GPIOB | 16 | Input (Hi-Z) | UART2/3 TX/RX, SPI1, I2C2, TMR2/3 |
| GPIOC | 16 | Input (Hi-Z) | TMR4/5, PWM0/1, CAN (future), misc AF |

### 5.2 Pinmux Strategy
- Each GPIO pad has at least 4 alternate function (AF) slots managed by NC_GPIO AF matrix.
- UART, SPI, I2C, Timer, PWM outputs are exclusively assigned through pinmux; no dedicated IO pads.
- SWDCLK and SWDIO are dedicated pads (not GPIOs) wired directly to Hazard3 debug port.
- nRESET and CLK_IN are dedicated padframe pads.

---

## 6. Peripheral Specifications

### 6.1 UART (4 instances)
- Full-duplex asynchronous serial
- Baud rate: up to 5 Mbit/s at 80 MHz clock
- FIFO: 8-deep TX/FIFO (per NC_UART spec)
- Hardware flow control (RTS/CTS) via GPIO AF
- IrDA encoder/decoder support on UART0 (pinmux)
- IRQ: TX empty, RX ready, framing error, overrun

### 6.2 SPI (2 instances)
- Master/slave configurable
- Clock polarity (CPOL) and phase (CPHA) modes 0–3
- 8- or 16-bit data frames
- Up to 40 Mbit/s at 80 MHz
- Hardware NSS management

### 6.3 I2C (3 instances — I2C2 is derivative addition)
- Standard (100 kHz), Fast (400 kHz), Fast-Plus (1 MHz)
- 7-bit and 10-bit addressing
- Multi-master capable
- SMBus / PMBus compatible
- I2C2: identical spec to I2C0/I2C1; added to satisfy derivative requirement R-006

### 6.4 Timers (6 instances)
- **TMR0–TMR3:** 16-bit general-purpose, up/down/center-aligned count
- **TMR4–TMR5:** 16-bit advanced control (additional PWM channels, dead-time insertion)
- All timers: prescaler, capture, compare, input filter, IRQ on overflow/compare/capture

### 6.5 PWM (2 instances)
- Complementary output pairs with dead-time
- 16-bit resolution
- Fault input for motor control applications

### 6.6 DMA Controller (1 instance, 8 channels)
- Memory-to-memory, peripheral-to-memory, memory-to-peripheral
- Fixed / incrementing address modes
- Channel priority arbitration
- Transfer complete and error interrupts

### 6.7 AES (1 instance)
- AES-128 and AES-256
- ECB, CBC, CTR, GCM modes
- DMA-assisted bulk encryption

### 6.8 CRC (1 instance)
- CRC-8, CRC-16, CRC-32 polynomial selection
- Hardware acceleration, single-cycle result

### 6.9 WWDT (1 instance)
- Window lower/upper bounds configurable
- Early-warning interrupt before reset
- Independent clock domain (LFOSC or external)

### 6.10 RTC (1 instance)
- BCD calendar: seconds, minutes, hours, day, month, year
- Alarm A and Alarm B
- Periodic wakeup timer
- Backup domain retention through VDD brown-out

### 6.11 ADC — 1 × 12-bit SAR (mixed-signal block)
- **Note:** The second ADC instance from the MSPM33C321A reference is removed per R-005.
- The remaining ADC instance is a 12-bit successive-approximation converter.
- This block is realised at the mixed-signal / custom-cell level; it is outside the NC digital RTL framework.
- ADC integration signals (start-of-conversion, end-of-conversion, IRQ) connect to the digital APB bus via a thin digital wrapper to be specified at integration stage.
- Channels: 8 external (via GPIO analog mode), 2 internal (temperature sensor, VREF)

---

## 7. Clock Architecture

| Domain | Source | Frequency |
|--------|--------|-----------|
| CPU core | External crystal / PLL via MS_CLK_RST | Up to 80 MHz |
| AHB bus | Same as CPU core | Up to 80 MHz |
| APB peripherals | AHB / 2 divider | Up to 40 MHz |
| WWDT, RTC | Low-frequency oscillator (LFOSC ~32 kHz) | 32 kHz |
| SWD debug | Separate SWCLK pad | External |

---

## 8. Reset Architecture

| Reset Source | Type | Propagation |
|-------------|------|------------|
| External nRESET pad | Active-low, async assert | Full chip reset |
| Power-on reset (POR) | Internal | Full chip reset |
| Software reset (SYSRESETREQ) | CPU register | Core + peripheral reset |
| WWDT timeout | Watchdog expiry | Full chip reset |
| Brown-out detector | Undervoltage | Core reset (IO pads hold) |

---

## 9. Power Domains

| Domain | Rail | Nominal Voltage | Scope |
|--------|------|----------------|-------|
| VCCD | VDD | 1.8 V | CPU core, SRAM, bus fabric |
| VSSD | GND | 0 V | Core ground |
| VDDIO | VDDIO | 3.3 V | IO pads, GPIO output drivers |
| VSSIO | VSSIO | 0 V | IO ground |
| VDDA | VDDA | 3.3 V | ADC, VREF, analog blocks |
| VSSA | VSSA | 0 V | Analog ground |

---

## 10. Package

### 10.1 Primary: LQFP-64

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Body | 10 mm × 10 mm |
| Lead count | 64 |
| Lead pitch | 0.5 mm |
| IO signal pads | 48 (GPIO × 3 ports) |
| Dedicated pads | 4 (nRESET, CLK_IN, SWDCLK, SWDIO) |
| Power pads | 8 (VDD×2, VSS×2, VDDIO×2, VDDA, VSSA) |
| Operating temp | −40 °C to +125 °C |

### 10.2 Alternate: VQFN-64

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Body | 9 mm × 9 mm |
| Lead count | 64 |
| Exposed pad | 1 (thermal die-attach / VSS) |
| IO signal pads | 48 |
| Dedicated pads | 4 |
| Power pads | 8 |

---

## 11. Derivative Delta Summary

This section explicitly documents every change from the MSPM33C321A reference:

| Change | Reference | Derivative | Impact |
|--------|-----------|------------|--------|
| Flash (NVM) size | 1 MB | **512 KB** | Reduced XIP address window, smaller BOM cost |
| SRAM size | 64 KB | **128 KB** | Doubled data memory; upgraded for real-time applications |
| ADC instances | 2 × 12-bit SAR | **1 × 12-bit SAR** | ADC1 removed; frees IRQ vector; reduces die area |
| I2C instances | 2 | **3** | I2C2 added; new IRQ vector assigned; extra APB slot consumed |
| All other subsystems | Unchanged | Unchanged | Maintained for design continuity and pinout compatibility |

---

## 12. Compliance Notes

- The derivative RTL is generated using NC_SOCGEN with the `mspm33c321a_derivative` SoC configuration.
- RISC-V Hazard3 is an architectural functional equivalent of ARM Cortex-M0+ but is a distinct ISA implementation; end-user firmware requires recompilation for RISC-V (RV32I toolchain).
- ADC and analog blocks are outside the NC_SOCGEN digital generation scope and require separate mixed-signal integration.
- DFT (scan insertion, ATPG) is to be specified in the DFT change notes artifact (separate document).

# Padframe Requirements — MSPM33C321A Derivative MCU

**Design Name:** `mspm33c321a_derivative`
**Date:** 2026-05-29
**Source:** `docs/requirements.md`, `docs/specifications.md`

---

## 1. Overview

This document defines the IO ring (padframe) requirements for the `mspm33c321a_derivative`. The padframe must accommodate 48 GPIO signal pads (3 × 16-pin GPIO ports), 4 dedicated function pads, and 8 power pads, totalling 60 functional pads within the LQFP-64 package body. The remaining 4 pad positions are allocated to corner/filler pads.

---

## 2. GPIO Count and Grouping

### 2.1 GPIO Pad Count

| Port | Pins | Description |
|------|------|-------------|
| GPIOA | 16 | Port A (PA[15:0]) — NC_GPIO instance 0 |
| GPIOB | 16 | Port B (PB[15:0]) — NC_GPIO instance 1 |
| GPIOC | 16 | Port C (PC[15:0]) — NC_GPIO instance 2 |
| **Total GPIO** | **48** | All bidirectional, configurable drive strength |

### 2.2 Grouping Assumptions

- Each 16-pin GPIO port maps to one `nc_gpio` peripheral instance.
- All GPIO pads are bidirectional (general-purpose IO) with full alternate function support.
- Pads support push-pull output, open-drain output, and input-only modes.
- Ports are distributed around the padframe to minimise signal routing distance to their primary peripheral assignment:
  - **GPIOA** — North/East sides: UART0/1, SPI0, I2C0/1 area
  - **GPIOB** — East/South sides: UART2/3, SPI1, I2C2 area
  - **GPIOC** — South/West sides: TMR4/5, PWM0/1, misc area
- Pad ordering within each port follows ascending pin number (P_0 at the lower-index pad position).

### 2.3 Alternate Function Pinmux (per pad)

Each GPIO pad in the padframe exposes:
- `core_gpio_N_out` — output data from nc_gpio AF matrix
- `core_gpio_N_oe_n` — output enable (active-LOW to padframe)
- `core_gpio_N_in` — sampled input data to nc_gpio
- `core_gpio_N_dm_[2:0]` — drive-mode select (strong/weak/open-drain)
- `core_gpio_N_inp_dis` — input path disable (for pure output / analog mode)
- `core_gpio_N_enable_h` — pad enable (active-HIGH)
- `core_gpio_N_slow` — slew-rate limiter enable

---

## 3. Dedicated Function Pads

| Pad Name | Direction | Function | Notes |
|----------|-----------|----------|-------|
| `PAD_NRESET` | Input | External reset, active-low | Schmitt trigger; glitch-filtered; connects to reset synchronizer |
| `PAD_CLK_IN` | Input | External clock/crystal input | Low-jitter clock pad; typically connected to crystal oscillator |
| `PAD_SWDCLK` | Input | SWD clock (SWD debug) | Dedicated Hazard3 SWD interface; no GPIO function |
| `PAD_SWDIO` | Bidirectional | SWD data (SWD debug) | Dedicated Hazard3 SWD interface; no GPIO function |

---

## 4. Power Pads

### 4.1 Required Power Pads

| Pad Name | Count | Rail | Nominal Voltage | Scope |
|----------|-------|------|----------------|-------|
| `VDD` | 2 | VCCD | 1.8 V | CPU core, SRAM, bus logic |
| `VSS` | 2 | VSSD | 0 V | Core ground |
| `VDDIO` | 2 | VDDIO | 3.3 V | GPIO IO ring drivers |
| `VSSIO` | 2 | VSSIO | 0 V | IO ground return |
| `VDDA` | 1 | VDDA | 3.3 V | ADC, VREF, analog subsystem |
| `VSSA` | 1 | VSSA | 0 V | Analog ground (isolated from VSSIO) |
| **Total** | **10** | | | |

> **Rationale:** Dual VDD/VSS pads reduce IR-drop across the core power mesh at 80 MHz operation. Dual VDDIO/VSSIO pads split IO ring power delivery symmetrically around the die perimeter. VDDA/VSSA are isolated from digital power for ADC noise performance. Total power pad count: 10.

### 4.2 Power Pad Distribution Strategy

```
             [North side]
   VDD  VDDIO  GPIO_A[15:8]  ...  GPIO_A[7:0]  SWDCLK
   |                                               |
[W]                                              [E]
   |                                               |
   VSS  VSSIO  GPIO_B[15:8]  ...  GPIO_B[7:0]  SWDIO
             [South side]
   VDDA  VSSA  GPIO_C[15:8]  ...  GPIO_C[7:0]  PAD_NRESET
   VDD   VDDIO  ...                            CLK_IN
   VSS   VSSIO
```

> Power pads are distributed roughly symmetrically: one VDD/VSS pair on the North side, one pair on the South side; one VDDIO/VSSIO pair on the North side, one pair on the South side; VDDA/VSSA on one corner group.

---

## 5. Package-Fit Assumptions and Constraints

### 5.1 Primary Package: LQFP-64

| Parameter | Value | Constraint |
|-----------|-------|-----------|
| Total package pins | 64 | Fixed — 64-lead LQFP |
| IO signal pads (GPIO) | 48 | 3 ports × 16 pins |
| Dedicated signal pads | 4 | nRESET, CLK_IN, SWDCLK, SWDIO |
| Power pads | 10 | 2×VDD/VSS + 2×VDDIO/VSSIO + VDDA/VSSA |
| Corner / filler pads | 2 | LQFP corner cells for structural ring closure |
| **Total occupied** | **64** | Fits exactly in LQFP-64 |

> **Constraint:** The total number of padframe cells (signal + power + corner) must not exceed 64. With 48 GPIO + 4 dedicated + 10 power + 2 corner = 64. ✓

### 5.2 Alternate Package: VQFN-64

Same pin count and signal assignment; the thermal pad (exposed die-attach pad) is tied to VSS and acts as an additional ground connection but does not occupy a padframe cell position.

### 5.3 Pad Technology Assumptions

- All digital IO pads: sky130 / target PDK general-purpose IO cell (bidirectional, 3.3 V tolerant, configurable drive strength: 2 mA / 4 mA / 8 mA / 12 mA).
- Power pads: PDK power-clamp cells with ESD protection.
- Clock input pad: specialised low-jitter analog-input cell to minimise clock skew and reduce phase noise injection.
- Reset pad: Schmitt-trigger input with internal pull-up (10 kΩ typical).
- SWD pads: weak pull-up on SWDIO, no pull on SWDCLK (driven by external debug probe).

### 5.4 Padframe Ring Closure

- 4 corner pad cells required for physical LQFP ring integrity.
- Filler / breaker cells may be inserted as required by `padframe-gen` to achieve DRC-clean ring.
- Bond wire to pad pitch: 0.5 mm (LQFP-64 standard).

---

## 6. ESD and Latch-up Requirements

| Parameter | Requirement |
|-----------|------------|
| HBM ESD | ≥ 2 kV (all IO pads) |
| CDM ESD | ≥ 250 V (all IO pads) |
| Latch-up immunity | > 100 mA trigger current at +85 °C |
| Power clamp | Required on all supply domains (VDD, VDDIO, VDDA) |
| IO clamp diodes | Rail-to-rail protection diodes on all signal pads |

---

## 7. Derivative Impact on Padframe

The padframe changes relative to the reference MSPM33C321A are minimal, consistent with the "small delta" scope:

| Change | Reference | Derivative | Padframe Impact |
|--------|-----------|------------|----------------|
| ADC1 removed | 2 analog mux inputs | 1 analog mux input | ADC1 analog pad group not needed; those GPIO pads become standard digital IO |
| I2C2 added | 2 I2C pinmux channels | 3 I2C pinmux channels | No new pads — I2C2 is assigned to existing GPIOB alternate functions; zero additional pad count |
| Flash 512 KB | 1 MB address | 512 KB address | No padframe impact (internal bus only) |
| SRAM 128 KB | 64 KB | 128 KB | No padframe impact (internal bus only) |

**Net padframe delta:** Zero additional pads required. I2C2 is absorbed into existing GPIOB alternate-function matrix. ADC1 removal frees no pads (GPIO pads remain; their analog-enable signal is de-asserted).

---

## 8. Padframe Configuration Summary (for `padframe-gen`)

```yaml
# Padframe summary — will be formalised in padframe_config.yaml at Stage 3
padframe:
  die_package: LQFP64
  total_pads: 64
  io_signal_pads: 52      # 48 GPIO + 4 dedicated
  power_pads: 10          # VDD×2 + VSS×2 + VDDIO×2 + VSSIO×2 + VDDA + VSSA
  corner_pads: 2

  gpio_ports:
    - name: gpioa
      width: 16
      side: north_east
      reset_oe: input
    - name: gpiob
      width: 16
      side: east_south
      reset_oe: input
    - name: gpioc
      width: 16
      side: south_west
      reset_oe: input

  dedicated_pads:
    - name: nreset
      type: schmitt_input
      pullup: 10k
    - name: clk_in
      type: analog_input
      pullup: none
    - name: swdclk
      type: digital_input
      pullup: none
    - name: swdio
      type: bidirectional
      pullup: weak

  power_pads:
    - name: VDD
      count: 2
      rail: vccd
      voltage: 1.8V
    - name: VSS
      count: 2
      rail: vssd
      voltage: 0V
    - name: VDDIO
      count: 2
      rail: vddio
      voltage: 3.3V
    - name: VSSIO
      count: 2
      rail: vssio
      voltage: 0V
    - name: VDDA
      count: 1
      rail: vdda
      voltage: 3.3V
    - name: VSSA
      count: 1
      rail: vssa
      voltage: 0V
```

schema_version: 1

# ============================================================
# HEADER
# ============================================================
header:
  author: NC MCU Factory Requirements Agent
  copyright_holder: NC Factory
  license: Apache-2.0

# ============================================================
# SOC
# ============================================================
# Design: mspm33c321a_derivative
# Reference: Texas Instruments MSPM33C321A
# Derivative delta:
#   - Flash: 1MB → 512KB   (R-003)
#   - SRAM:  64KB → 128KB  (R-004)
#   - ADC:   2 → 1 instance (mixed-signal; outside NC_SOCGEN RTL) (R-005)
#   - I2C:   2 → 3 instances (+1 per brief) (R-006)
# ============================================================
soc:
  name: mspm33c321a_derivative
  top_module: mspm33c321a_derivative_compute_ss

  # Hazard3 RV32I — functional equivalent of ARM Cortex-M0+
  # Selected because:
  #   • NC framework is RISC-V only (D-001)
  #   • hazard3_2port_swd preserves 2-pin SWD debug (R-020)
  #   • 2-port variant enables XIP + SRAM simultaneous access
  cpu_type: hazard3_2port_swd

  # Package must exactly match package.name below
  package: LQFP64

  # 80 MHz — reference device spec (R-002)
  clock_hz: 80000000

  reset_active_low: true

  # hazard3_internal: built-in PLIC handles all peripheral IRQs (D-002)
  # nc_pic is NOT used with hazard3 CPU types
  interrupt_policy: hazard3_internal

# ============================================================
# MEMORY
# ============================================================
memory:
  # Flash: 512 KB (R-003 — reduced from reference 1MB)
  xip:
    enabled: true
    base_addr: 0x00000000
    size_bytes: 0x00080000    # 512 KB
    module: nc_xip

  # SRAM: 128 KB (R-004 — upgraded from reference 64KB)
  # Implemented as 4 × CF_SRAM_1024x32 banks (4 × 32KB = 128KB)
  # CF_SRAM_1024x32 included in link_IPs.json (D-008)
  sram:
    base_addr: 0x20000000
    size_bytes: 0x00020000    # 128 KB — power of 2 ✓
    module: nc_ahb_to_sram
    interface: ahb_lite
    model: cf
    sim_model: cf

# ============================================================
# FABRIC
# ============================================================
fabric:
  ahb:
    reserved_region_value: 0xBADB00B5
    # DMAC requires AHB master port — enabled for 8-channel DMA (R-013)
    with_dmac: true
    dmac_priority: true

  apb_auto:
    base_addr_start: 0x40000000
    slot_size: 0x1000         # 4 KB per peripheral slot
    slots_per_bus: 16
    # 26 peripheral APB slots needed → 2 buses required (D-004)
    min_buses: 2
    bus_stride: 0x00010000
    name_prefix: apb

  irq_auto:
    start: 0

  dma_auto:
    start: 0

  passthrough:
    clk_rst_pwr: true
    io_cfg_ss: true

# ============================================================
# PACKAGE
# ============================================================
# LQFP-64: 48 GPIO signal pads + 4 dedicated + 10 power = 62 + 2 corner = 64
# See docs/padframe_requirements.md for full pad breakdown
package:
  name: LQFP64
  io_pads: 52      # 48 GPIO + 4 dedicated (nRESET, CLK_IN, SWDCLK, SWDIO)
  power_pins: 10   # VDD×2, VSS×2, VDDIO×2, VSSIO×2, VDDA×1, VSSA×1

  # 3 × 16-pin GPIO ports — 48 GPIO total (R-018)
  gpio_ports:
    - name: gpioa
      width: 16
      reset_oe: input    # Safe power-on default: all pads high-impedance (D-011)
    - name: gpiob
      width: 16
      reset_oe: input
    - name: gpioc
      width: 16
      reset_oe: input

# ============================================================
# PERIPHERALS
# ============================================================
# Total APB slots used: 3+4+2+3+6+2+1+1+1+1+1+1 = 26
# Mapped to 2 APB buses (16 slots each = 32 total, 6 spare)
#
# ADC note (R-005): The remaining 12-bit SAR ADC instance is a
# mixed-signal block outside NC_SOCGEN. It is NOT listed here.
# Its digital interface (APB wrapper + IRQ) is to be added at
# physical integration stage.
peripherals:

  # --- GPIO (R-018): 3 ports × 16 pins = 48 GPIO ---
  - type: gpio
    module: nc_gpio
    count: 3            # gpioa (inst 0), gpiob (inst 1), gpioc (inst 2)
    instance_prefix: gpio

  # --- UART (R-009): 4 instances, unchanged from reference ---
  - type: uart
    module: nc_uart
    count: 4            # uart0–uart3
    instance_prefix: uart

  # --- SPI (R-010): 2 instances, unchanged from reference ---
  - type: spi
    module: nc_spi
    count: 2            # spi0–spi1
    instance_prefix: spi

  # --- I2C (R-006): 3 instances — reference 2 + derivative +1 ---
  # i2c2 is the new instance added per the derivative brief
  - type: i2c
    module: nc_i2c
    count: 3            # i2c0, i2c1 (reference), i2c2 (NEW — derivative addition)
    instance_prefix: i2c

  # --- General-Purpose Timers (R-011): 4 × 16-bit GP ---
  # --- Advanced Timers (R-012): 2 × 16-bit with PWM/dead-time ---
  # Both GP and advanced timers use nc_tmr; total 6 instances
  - type: tmr
    module: nc_tmr
    count: 6            # tmr0–tmr3 (GP), tmr4–tmr5 (advanced)
    instance_prefix: tmr

  # --- PWM (R-012): 2 instances from advanced timer outputs ---
  - type: pwm
    module: nc_pwm
    count: 2            # pwm0–pwm1
    instance_prefix: pwm

  # --- DMA Controller (R-013): 1 controller, 8 channels ---
  # Requires fabric.ahb.with_dmac: true (set above)
  - type: dmac
    module: nc_dmac
    count: 1
    instance_prefix: dmac

  # --- CRC Accelerator (R-014) ---
  - type: crc
    module: nc_crc
    count: 1
    instance_prefix: crc

  # --- AES Accelerator (R-015): AES-128/256 ---
  - type: aes
    module: nc_aes
    count: 1
    instance_prefix: aes

  # --- Windowed Watchdog Timer (R-016) ---
  - type: wwdt
    module: nc_wwdt
    count: 1
    instance_prefix: wwdt

  # --- Real-Time Clock (R-017): calendar + alarm ---
  - type: rtc
    module: nc_rtc
    count: 1
    instance_prefix: rtc

  # --- SysTick (D-009 / NC infrastructure) ---
  # ARM-compatible SysTick; required for RTOS tick
  - type: systick
    module: nc_systick
    count: 1
    instance_prefix: systick

  # NOTE: nc_pic is NOT included — hazard3_internal policy uses
  # the Hazard3 built-in PLIC for all interrupt management (D-002)

# ============================================================
# GENERATION
# ============================================================
generation:
  emit_padframe_handoff: true     # Required for pipeline beyond Stage 1 (nc_chipgen)
  emit_fw_pack: true
  emit_smoke: true
  emit_directed_tests: true
  emit_datasheet: true
  emit_csv: true
  emit_manifest: true
  emit_filelist: true
  emit_reg_spec: true
  smoke_sequence_format: verilog_include_calls
  verilog_standard: verilog-2005
  include_comments: true
  build_fw_smoke: true
  fw_signature_addr: 0x20000000
